title: Beyond the Markovian limit: Exact solutions for active motion in a power-law viscoelastic bath
authors: Mintu Karmakar, Jure Dobnikar, Ignacio Pagonabarraga
abstract: Active particles from bacteria to synthetic microswimmers often navigate viscoelastic media with complex relaxation dynamics. The classical active Brownian model that assumes instantaneous friction is clearly not applicable to describe such motility, while the non-Markovian processes combined with viscoelasticity are relatively unexplored. Here, we develop an analytical theory for an active particle in a power-law viscoelastic medium by solving coupled non-Markovian generalized Langevin equations for translational and rotational degrees of freedom. The viscoelastic memory results in novel phenomena such as fractional short-time transport, enhanced long-time persistence, and de-correlation of the instantaneous force and the swimmer orientation. We demonstrate that the memory kernel controls the anomalous scaling exponents, while the activity determines the crossover between sub-diffusive, ballistic and diffusive regimes. Our work provides a framework for theoretical description of biological and synthetic micro swimmers in complex biological and polymeric environments.
summary: This paper provides an analytical theory for active particles moving in viscoelastic fluids whose friction dynamics are described by power-law memory. By solving non-Markovian Langevin equations, the authors predict novel phenomena like stretched exponential decay of orientation and a measurable time-delay between propulsion force and particle direction. This framework is crucial for understanding biological swimmers in complex polymeric environments.
main_problem: The study addresses active particle motion in viscoelastic media, moving beyond simplified Markovian friction models that fail to capture complex relaxation dynamics.
main_result: The theory derives analytical expressions showing that the memory kernel controls anomalous scaling exponents and introduces a measurable time-delay between propulsion force and swimmer orientation.
method: The authors developed an analytical framework by solving coupled, non-Markovian Generalized Langevin Equations (GLEs) for translational and rotational motion.
model_or_system: The model describes a self-propelled microswimmer moving in a 2D viscoelastic bath characterized by power-law memory kernels ($\Gamma(t) \sim t^{-\alpha}$). The dynamics are governed by coupled GLEs incorporating both activity ($v_0$) and viscoelastic damping.
key_observables: Orientational Correlation Function $C(	ilde{t})$, Mean-Square Displacement (MSD), and the Memory Delay Function $d(t)$.
important_parameters: Memory exponents ($\alpha_T, \alpha_R$), activity strength ($Pe$), and intrinsic rotation frequency ($\omega_0$). The key regimes include $\alpha=1$ (Newtonian) versus $0 < \alpha < 1$ (power-law memory).
main_assumptions: The analysis operates in the overdamped limit, assumes zero-mean Gaussian noise processes consistent with the Fluctuation-Dissipation Theorem.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the stretched exponential decay of $C(	ilde{t})$ for $\alpha < 1$, and show the dependence of the memory delay function $d(t)$ on the viscoelastic exponents ($\alpha_R, \alpha_T$).
paper_structure: The paper develops the GLEs, solves them using Laplace transforms to find correlation functions ($C(t)$), derives expressions for $\langle\Delta r(t)angle$ and MSD, and finally analyzes the non-Markovian coupling via the memory delay function $d(t)$.
why_interesting: While focused on soft matter, the mathematical framework of solving coupled, non-Markovian GLEs with power-law memory kernels is directly applicable to modeling open quantum systems where environmental dissipation (bath interaction) exhibits anomalous or long-range correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Orientational correlation function 𝐶(˜𝑡) = ⟨ˆ𝑛(˜𝑡) · ˆ𝑛(0)⟩as a function of dimensionless time ˜𝑡= 𝑡/𝜏𝑅for different memory exponents 𝛼= 𝛼𝑅= 𝛼𝑇. (a) Moderate intrinsic angular velocity ˜𝜔0 = 1. (b) Strong intrinsic angular velocity ˜𝜔0 = 5. The dashed black curve corresponds to the Newtonian limit (𝛼= 1), which exhibits exponential decay. For 𝛼< 1, the decay becomes stretched exponential, producing long-lived orientational persistence. Increasing memory (smaller 𝛼) enhances the lifetime of oscillatory correlations without altering the intrinsic oscillation frequency. (c,d) Corresponding short-time behaviour of 𝐶(˜𝑡) in a linear scale exhibits rapid decay from 1 for...

The dashed black curve corresponds to the Newtonian case (𝛼= 1). Stronger viscoelastic memory (smaller 𝛼) enhances orientational coherence and enlarges spiral excursions.

Translational memory produces fractional short-time scaling (∼˜𝑡𝛼𝑇), while ro- tational memory prolongs ballistic motion and enhances oscillatory transport signatures for chiral swimmers.

FIG. 5. (Color online) (a) Delay function ˜𝑑(˜𝑡) for different memory exponents 𝛼= 𝛼𝑅= 𝛼𝑇. The delay vanishes in the Newtonian limit (𝛼= 1) and develops a pronounced peak as memory strengthens. (b) Peak delay 𝑑peak = max[ ˜𝑑(˜𝑡)] in the (𝛼𝑅, 𝛼𝑇) plane. The delay is maximized when both translational and rotational memory are strong, demonstrating cooperative enhancement of force-orientation lag.
